section.title: Lord, You Have Been Our Dwelling Place
section.sectionRationale: A prayer of Moses that opens Book IV of the Psalter, this psalm contrasts God's eternity with the brevity and frailty of human life under sin's judgment, then prays for wisdom to number our days, a prayer answered fully in Christ, the eternal One who became our true and lasting dwelling place.
section.thesis: Moses contrasts the everlasting God, who was our dwelling place before the mountains were born, with mortal life that passes like grass, and prays for a heart of wisdom to live well in light of both God's eternity and human brevity, a wisdom fulfilled in Christ who conquers the death this psalm laments.
section.frameNote: Four movements: God as our everlasting dwelling place, verses 1-2; the brevity of human life under God's hand, verses 3-6; the reality of divine anger over sin, verses 7-11; and a prayer for wisdom, joy, and enduring work, verses 12-17.

verses.text: Lord, You have been our dwelling place through all generations.
Before the mountains were born or You brought forth the earth and the world, from {{olam|everlasting}} to everlasting You are God.
You return man to dust, saying, "Return, O sons of mortals."
For in Your sight a thousand years are but a day that passes, or a watch of the night.
You sweep them away in their sleep; they are like the new grass of the morning,
in the morning it springs up new, but by evening it fades and withers.

units.label: Consumed by Your Anger
units.note: the reality of divine judgment on sin

verses.text: For we are consumed by Your anger and terrified by Your wrath.
You have set our iniquities before You, our secret sins in the light of Your presence.
For all our days decline in Your fury; we finish our years with a sigh.
The length of our days is seventy years, or eighty if we are strong, yet their pride is but labor and sorrow, for they quickly pass, and we fly away.
Who knows the power of Your anger? Your wrath matches the fear You are due.

units.label: Teach Us to Number Our Days
units.note: a prayer for wisdom, joy, and enduring work

verses.text: So teach us to number our days, that we may present a heart of {{chokmah|wisdom}}.
Return, O LORD! How long will it be? Have compassion on Your servants.
Satisfy us in the morning with Your {{chesed|loving devotion}}, that we may sing for joy and be glad all our days.
Make us glad for as many days as You have afflicted us, for as many years as we have seen evil.
May Your work be shown to Your servants, and Your splendor to their children.
May the {{noam|favor}} of the Lord our God rest upon us; establish for us the work of our hands, yes, establish the work of our hands!

olam.gloss: Everlasting, from ancient time to distant future, a span beyond human measure. God alone existed before creation and will exist after it, from everlasting to everlasting.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, skill in living rightly before God. Moses prays that numbering our brief days would produce not mere information but a heart genuinely shaped by this practical, godly wisdom.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast, covenant love. Moses prays that this love, freshly given each morning, would be the true satisfaction that outweighs the toil and sorrow of mortal life.

noam.gloss: Favor, pleasantness, beauty. Moses closes by praying that this quality of God Himself would rest upon His people, giving lasting worth to work that would otherwise vanish like grass.

basic.context: Moses prays this psalm during Israel's long years in the wilderness, where an entire generation died under God's judgment for unbelief. He contrasts God's eternity, unshaken before the mountains existed, with human life, which passes like grass, cut down by evening.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes a specific season of national judgment in the wilderness, and it prescribes an abiding wisdom for every generation: to number our fleeting days honestly before God so that we might live wisely rather than wasting the little time we are given.
basic.originalLanguages: God is olam, everlasting, existing before the mountains were born, verse 2. Moses prays for a heart of chokmah, wisdom, verse 12, that flows from honestly numbering our days, and pleads for God's chesed, steadfast love, verse 14, and noam, favor or pleasantness, verse 17, to rest upon His people's brief and toilsome lives.
basic.typology: The psalm's opening confession, that the LORD Himself, not any place, has been our dwelling place through all generations, points forward to Christ, in whom believers dwell and who dwells in them, the true and lasting refuge beyond every earthly and temporal shelter.
basic.christ: Moses laments a lifespan swallowed by labor, sorrow, and death, the wages of sin, Romans 6:23. Christ enters fully into this frailty, taking on mortal flesh, and by His resurrection breaks the power of death this psalm mourns, so that the labor of those in Him is never in vain, 1 Corinthians 15:54-58.
basic.god: God is shown as eternal, existing before all creation, and as righteously angry at human sin, yet also as the God of compassion who can be asked to relent, to satisfy His people with love, and to establish the fleeting work of their hands with lasting significance.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Peter 3:8 directly cites this psalm's picture of a thousand years as a day in God's sight. James 4:14 echoes the fleeting nature of human life as a mist. 1 Corinthians 15:54-58 answers this psalm's lament over death with the promise of resurrection victory in Christ.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's contrast between God's eternity and human frailty, and its prayer that fleeting, toilsome lives would be established with lasting purpose, is answered in Christ, the eternal Son who entered our mortality, conquered death, and makes the work of His people in Him truly everlasting.

context.literary: A prayer of Moses in four movements, moving from God's eternity, verses 1-2, to human frailty under judgment, verses 3-11, to a closing petition for wisdom, mercy, and enduring purpose, verses 12-17, opening Book IV of the Psalter.
context.historical: Composed during or reflecting on Israel's forty years of wandering, when an entire generation, under God's judgment for unbelief at Kadesh-barnea, died in the wilderness without entering the promised land, Numbers 14:29-35.
context.canonical: As the opening psalm of Book IV, following the crisis and unresolved longing that closed Book III in Psalm 89, this psalm reorients Israel's hope away from any earthly throne and back to the eternal LORD Himself as the true dwelling place of His people across every generation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confess God's eternal, unshakeable being in contrast to human mortality under sin's judgment, and to pray for the wisdom that comes from honestly facing life's brevity, together with a plea for God's compassion and lasting purpose.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes Israel's specific experience of wilderness judgment and mortality; it prescribes for every generation the abiding practice of numbering our days honestly and seeking God's favor to establish our necessarily limited work.

debates.question: Does the 'seventy years, or eighty if we are strong' of verse 10 set a fixed, universal limit on human lifespan for all generations?

views.label: It describes a general, typical human lifespan rather than an absolute limit
views.case: The verse speaks descriptively of ordinary human experience under mortality; Moses himself lived to 120, Deuteronomy 34:7, showing the number is not presented as an inviolable ceiling.

views.label: It reflects the specific, shortened lifespans of the wilderness generation under God's judgment
views.case: Coming directly after verses describing God's wrath consuming that generation's days, the seventy or eighty years may specifically describe the judged wilderness generation rather than humanity in every age.

debates.bearing: Either way, the psalm's larger point stands undiminished: human life, however long, is brief and fleeting before the eternal God, and calls for wisdom rather than presumption about the days we are given.

study.originalLanguages: God is confessed as olam, everlasting, from before the mountains existed to beyond all ages, verse 2. Moses asks God to teach His people to number their days so that they gain a heart of chokmah, wisdom, verse 12, and prays for chesed, steadfast love, verse 14, and noam, favor, verse 17, to rest upon a people whose days otherwise vanish like grass.

typology.type: The LORD as our dwelling place through all generations, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ, in whom believers abide and who Himself is their true refuge and home, John 15:4, Hebrews 13:8.

typology.type: Establishing the fleeting work of our hands, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's finished work, which secures that labor done in the Lord is never in vain, 1 Corinthians 15:58.

study.christ: Moses mourns a mortal life consumed by God's righteous anger against sin, the wages of sin being death, Romans 6:23. Christ, the eternal Son, entered fully into human frailty, and by His death and resurrection broke the power of death this psalm laments, 1 Corinthians 15:54-57, so that those united to Him have a dwelling place, a wisdom, and an enduring work that outlasts every fleeting mortal day.
study.god: God is eternal, existing before the mountains were born and beyond every generation, yet He is also the righteous Judge whose anger at sin is real and terrifying. He is equally the compassionate LORD who can be asked to relent, to satisfy His people each morning with steadfast love, and to give their brief lives lasting purpose.

crossReferences.note: Directly cites this psalm's truth that a day is like a thousand years to the Lord, and a thousand years like a day.
Echoes this psalm's picture of human life as fleeting, like a mist that appears for a little while and vanishes.
Answers this psalm's lament over death and futile labor with the promise of resurrection victory and labor that is never in vain.

study.oneStory: Moses' prayer contrasts the eternal God with fleeting, sin-judged human life, and asks for wisdom, mercy, and enduring purpose. That prayer is answered in Christ, the eternal One who took on our mortality, conquered the death this psalm mourns, and became the true and lasting dwelling place of His people forever.

sermon.title: Frail As Summer's Flower
sermon.why: Preaching directly from Psalm 90, Begg contrasts the brevity of human life with God's eternity, showing that only trust in the provision God has made in Christ allows believers to number their days aright, the very prayer at the heart of this psalm.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe God in verses 1-2?

options.text: As the eternal dwelling place of His people, existing from everlasting to everlasting before creation.
options.note: These verses confess God's eternity, existing before the mountains or the earth were made.

options.text: As a God who came into being at creation.
options.note: The psalm explicitly says God existed before the mountains and the earth were formed.

options.text: As a distant, uninvolved deity.
options.note: The psalm describes an intimately involved God who is Himself a dwelling place for His people.

options.text: As the Creator of the mountains and the earth.
options.note: This is true, part of verse 2, though the fuller point of these verses is God's eternity from everlasting to everlasting, not merely His role as Creator.

quiz.q: What image does the psalm use in verses 5-6 to describe human life?

options.text: New grass that springs up in the morning but fades and withers by evening.
options.note: This vivid image captures the brief, fleeting nature of mortal life.

options.text: A mighty cedar that stands for centuries.
options.note: The image used is fragile grass, the opposite of an enduring cedar.

options.text: An unshakeable mountain.
options.note: Mountains represent God's eternity in verse 2, not the brevity of human life.

options.text: Something swept away suddenly, like a dream.
options.note: This echoes verse 5's 'You sweep them away,' but the specific and vivid image named in verses 5-6 is grass springing up new in the morning and withering by evening.

quiz.q: According to verse 10, what characterizes the length of human days?

options.text: About seventy or eighty years, yet marked by labor and sorrow, quickly passing.
options.note: The verse names a typical lifespan and describes it as full of toil that passes swiftly.

options.text: Hundreds of years for every person without exception.
options.note: The psalm describes a typical lifespan far shorter than centuries.

options.text: A life entirely free from trouble.
options.note: The verse specifically names labor and sorrow as characterizing these years.

options.text: A short span best measured in decades, not centuries.
options.note: This is essentially correct, though the verse gives a more specific figure, seventy years, or eighty if we are strong, together with its labor and sorrow.

quiz.q: What does Moses ask God to teach His people in verse 12?

options.text: To number their days, that they may gain a heart of wisdom.
options.note: This is the psalm's central petition, connecting honest awareness of life's brevity to genuine wisdom.

options.text: To ignore the passage of time entirely.
options.note: The prayer is for careful awareness of time, not for ignoring it.

options.text: To build a temple as quickly as possible.
options.note: The request concerns wisdom for living, not a building project.

options.text: To live each day with careful, deliberate purpose.
options.note: This is a fair summary of the prayer's intent, though the psalm's exact words ask specifically to number our days so that we gain a heart of wisdom.

quiz.q: How does Christ answer the lament of this psalm over mortality and futile labor?

options.text: By entering human frailty and, through His resurrection, breaking the power of death, so labor in Him is never in vain.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:54-58 directly answers this psalm's lament with the promise of resurrection victory in Christ.

options.text: By eliminating the need for any prayer about wisdom or mortality.
options.note: The New Testament deepens rather than eliminates the call to live wisely in light of life's brevity.

options.text: By extending every believer's earthly lifespan indefinitely.
options.note: Christ's answer is resurrection life and eternal purpose, not an indefinite extension of mortal years.

options.text: By assuring believers of resurrection life beyond the grave.
options.note: This is true and very close to correct, though the fullest connection is 1 Corinthians 15's specific declaration that death is swallowed up in victory through Christ, directly answering this psalm's lament.

questions: Moses confesses that God has been our dwelling place through all generations. Where else do you tend to look for security or permanence instead of God Himself?
The psalm compares human life to grass that withers by evening. How does remembering the brevity of your own life shape your priorities today?
Moses prays, 'Teach us to number our days, that we may present a heart of wisdom.' What would change in your schedule or decisions this week if you took life's brevity seriously?
Verse 14 asks God to satisfy us each morning with His steadfast love. What are you looking to for satisfaction instead of God's love at the start of your days?
The psalm closes praying that God would establish the work of our hands. What work or labor in your life do you long to know is not in vain, and how does Christ's resurrection answer that longing?

translationNotes.note: The heading, 'A prayer of Moses the man of God,' links this psalm to Israel's wilderness era, making it likely the oldest psalm in the Psalter.
This verse is directly cited in 2 Peter 3:8 to describe God's relationship to time as utterly unlike human experience of it.

Favor can also be rendered beauty, and the verse's closing repetition, 'establish the work of our hands, yes, establish the work of our hands,' emphasizes the psalmist's earnest longing for lasting purpose.

meta.notes: BSB text obtained via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com's BSB (Berean Standard Bible), cross-checked against the section map bounds (Psalm 90:1-17, 17 verses). This psalm opens Book IV of the Psalter (Psalms 90-106). Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's message 'Frail As Summer's Flower' on Psalm 90 at truthforlife.org.
